title: Chapter 18: The Files
content: The USB drive is small and black and unremarkable. A SanDisk, 64GB, the kind you'd buy in a multipack at an electronics store. I turn it over in my fingers. The plastic is cold. The control room is cold. Everything is cold.
I slide it into the laptop.
A prompt appears: **Enter password.**
I stare at the blinking cursor. The laptop hums beneath my palms, a low mechanical pulse, indifferent and steady. The screen casts blue-white light across my knuckles.
BLY.
**Incorrect.**
QUINT.
**Incorrect.**
PETER.
**Incorrect.**
My fingers hover. The cursor blinks. The hum of the machine fills the silence like tinnitus.
REBECCA.
**Incorrect.**

Five tries. The drive will lock me out after ten — I'm assuming, I don't know, I don't know anything about my father's security protocols or his paranoia or his mind. I don't know him at all. I came here thinking I did. I came here thinking Peter Bly was a man with a house and a secret and a set of rules I could learn, and instead he's —
The cursor blinks.

I close my eyes. I think of what Mrs. Grose said. *Your mother was the best person I've ever known.* I think of the photograph I never saw, the story I never heard, the woman who existed in the negative space of my father's silence. I think of a name I was never supposed to know.
CAROLINE.
The drive opens.
The file structure is immaculate.

That's the first thing I notice. Not the content — the organization. Peter Bly was a man who kept things in order. Folders nested inside folders, each one labeled in sober uppercase: ROUND 01, ROUND 02, ROUND 03, and on through ROUND 07. Inside each round: subfolders. RULES. OBSERVATIONS. INCIDENT LOGS. PHOTOGRAPHIC EVIDENCE. VIDEO. Inside those: dated files, timestamped to the minute. PDFs, spreadsheets, .mp4s, .docs. A taxonomy of control.
I click through them without opening anything yet. Just looking. Getting the shape of it.
At the top level, below the rounds, there's another set of folders. These are labeled with names.
SARAH — 2008.
CLAIRE — 2010.
HELEN — 2013.
JESSEL — 2018.
5TH CYCLE — 2021.
Five names. Five governesses. Fifteen years.

I pull my hand back from the trackpad as if it's burned me. The screen's glow catches the sweat on my palm. The control room's air recycler whispers — a sound like breathing, if you were the kind of person who heard breathing everywhere, and I am becoming that kind of person.
I open the first folder.

Sarah Ashworth. Age 26. Hired September 2008. Background: primary education, references from two private households. Psychological profile attached: 14-page PDF. I don't read it. Not yet. I look at the summary line instead.
*Cycle duration: 4 rounds. Status: PLAYER EXITED.*
There's an exit interview — no. There's a document labeled EXIT in Sarah's folder. I open it. It's not an interview. It's a debrief. Peter's notes on her departure, written in clinical third person.

*Player exhibited expected stress responses through Rounds 1–3. Round 4 (Gaslighting) produced acute decompensation. Player accessed exterior communication without authorization. Player removed herself from the property at 0347 hours on 11/02/2008. No attempt at retrieval. Cycle closed.*
That's it. She ran. Four rounds, and she ran. Good for Sarah Ashworth. Good for her.
My mouth tastes like copper. I swallow.
**CLAIRE — 2010**
Claire Hennessy. Age 31. Hired February 2010. Five rounds. Status line:
*Cycle duration: 5 rounds. Status: MEDICAL TERMINATION.*

There's a hospital transfer form. Injuries unspecified — "psychological emergency," the form says. Admitted to St. Anne's, overnight stay, released into the care of — the next line is redacted. Blacked out. But there's a timestamp. The admission was three hours after what Peter's incident log calls "Round 5 escalation event." No further details.
I don't open the video folder.
My hands are shaking. The laptop hums. The room smells like dust and old circuitry and something else, something under it — the ghost of a disinfectant that was never quite strong enough.
Helen Vale. Age 29. Hired October 2013. Six rounds.
*Cycle duration: 6 rounds. Status: RELOCATED.*
Relocated. I click through every subfolder. There's no exit document. No hospital form. No incident log beyond Round 6. Just a single text file that reads:
*RELOCATED. Coordinates redacted. No forwarding address. Cycle closed.*

The word *relocated* sits on my tongue like a stone. You relocate furniture. You relocate a desk. You don't relocate a person. A person moves. A person goes. *Relocated* means someone moved her. Someone decided where she went next. Someone made that choice for her.
The control room is cold. The cold is in my joints, in the meat of my thighs where they press the chair, in my fingers hovering over the keys.
**5TH CYCLE — 2021**

No name. The folder label is just the year. I open it. The psychological profile is there — 16 pages, same format as the others — but every instance of the subject's name has been replaced with **[REDACTED]**. Not blacked out. Replaced. Carefully, surgically removed, like a tumor.
*Cycle duration: 3 rounds. Status: TERMINATED — LEGAL.*

Legal. I open the LEGAL subfolder. There are documents — actual legal documents. A cease-and-desist. A non-disclosure agreement. A settlement figure with six digits, though the number itself is pixelated, corrupted, or deliberately obscured.

Someone fought back. Someone got out and lawyered up and made enough noise that Peter had to smother it with money and paperwork. Three rounds — she barely made it through Deception — but she got out, and she made it cost him.
Good for her. Good for the unnamed woman with the redacted name and the settlement and the NDA.
But she didn't go to the police. She didn't go public. She took the money and the silence and she disappeared into whatever life you can build after something like this.
The bile is in my throat now, acid-thin and warm. I swallow it down.
**JESSEL — 2018**
I don't want to open this folder. My hand is on the trackpad and I don't want to press. But I press.

Rebecca Jessel. Age 24. Hired June 2018. The file tells me things I already know, and then things I don't. Her psychological profile is the longest yet — 22 pages. Peter was thorough. He mapped her the way a cartographer maps terrain, finding the ridges and the valleys and the low places where water pools. He understood her better than she understood herself. That was the point.
*Cycle duration: 7 rounds. Status: INCOMPLETE.*
Not closed. Not terminated. Not relocated. **Incomplete.** As if she were a project abandoned mid-task. As if she simply failed to finish.
I open the video subfolder. There's one file inside.
JESSEL_LAKE_071218.mp4
23 seconds. Resolution: 720p. Camera: EAST LAKE — FIXED.
I click play.
The footage is night-vision green. The lake is black and white and silver, the surface flat and dark, and there — at the edge, where the lawn gives way to water — is a figure.
Jessel.

She's walking into the lake. Not quickly. Not slowly. The way you'd walk into the surf on a beach holiday, except this is November, this is England, this is a lake that goes down twenty feet in the center, and she is walking into it.
She stops. The water is at her knees. She turns.
She faces the camera.

Her face is wrong. That's the only word I have. Wrong. She's smiling. A wide smile, the kind you give to someone you love when you're trying to convince them you're fine, except there's no one in front of her, there's only the camera, and the camera doesn't need convincing. Her cheeks are wet. Her eyes are red. She's been crying — not the gentle weeping of sad movies, but the ugly kind, the kind that swells your face and cramps your throat, the kind that means you've been doing it for hours.
She raises one hand.
She waves.

It's a small wave. Lazy. Almost affectionate. The way you'd wave goodbye to someone from a train window when the train is already moving and you know you're not going to see them again and you've made your peace with it.
She turns away from the camera.
She walks deeper.
The footage cuts to static.
White noise. Twenty-three seconds, and the last eight are static. I stare at the snow on the screen and I hear the hum of the laptop and I hear the blood in my ears and I hear nothing at all.
I close the video.

I press my hands flat on the desk. The surface is cold. The metal of the desk leg is colder. I grip it until my knuckles ache. I breathe. The air tastes like dust and old plastic and something underneath — the trace memory of a woman's perfume, or maybe I'm imagining it. Maybe I'm imagining everything.
I'm not imagining the file.
There's one more folder at the top level.
GAME DESIGN.
I open it.

Inside is a single document: 47 pages, PDF, created October 14, 2009. The twins were seven years old. Peter would have been — I do the math — thirty-eight. In his prime. At the height of whatever conviction drove him.
I scroll to page one.

It's written like a white paper. Like a grant proposal. Like the kind of document a think tank produces when they want to sound serious and empirical about something monstrous. The font is Garamond. The margins are justified. The language is clean.
*On the Development of Resilience Through Structured Adversity: A Domestic Application*
I read it. I read all of it.

The argument is this: children who are sheltered from difficulty become fragile. Children who face uncontrolled trauma become broken. But children who face *controlled* adversity — adversity calibrated to their developmental stage, administered in progressive stages, supervised by a trained professional — these children become resilient. They become adaptable. They survive.

He cites studies. Bessel van der Kolk. The ACEs framework. The Stockholm resilience studies. He's done his reading. He's done it selectively, with the precision of a man building a case rather than seeking the truth, but he's done it. The references are real. The conclusions he draws from them are not.

The rounds are laid out in a table. Round 1: Isolation. Round 2: Observation. Round 3: Deception. Round 4: Gaslighting. Round 5: Dependency. Round 6: Violence. Round 7: — the cell is blank. No label. No description. Just a dash and then a footnote.
I scroll to the footnote.

*The final round must never be reached. If a player survives Round 7, the game resets. There is no winning condition. There is only continuation. The game exists because it exists. It is the thing that gives the house purpose.*
I read it again. The words don't change.
There is no winning condition.
I scroll down. There's an addendum. Dated 2016, seven years after the original document. Peter's own handwriting, scanned:
*Confirmation of trust fund incentive structure. Player engagement requires tangible objective. Fund designation: fictive. No disbursement mechanism exists. Purpose: motivational only.*
Fictive.
The trust fund isn't real.

I knew it — some part of me has known it since the twins first mentioned it, since the round structure crystallized into something with rules and stakes and a goal. But knowing something and reading it in your dead father's handwriting, in a PDF labeled GAME DESIGN, under the heading *fictive*, are different experiences entirely.

The trust fund is a lie. It was always a lie. It's the carrot at the end of every maze, the prize at the bottom of every cereal box, the thing you keep running toward because without it you'd have to admit you're running in circles. There is no payout. There is no exit. Jessel walked into a lake chasing money that didn't exist.
She drowned chasing a lie my father told.

The bile comes up. I make it to the wastebasket this time. It burns my throat and tastes like acid and stale coffee and something underneath, the underneath taste of the control room, the underneath taste of Bly, which is the taste of a house that eats people and calls it education.
I close the laptop.

The screen goes dark and the control room is black, absolutely black, the kind of darkness you only get in a room with no windows, a room buried inside a house that was designed to contain things. The hum of the laptop is the only sound. The cold is the only sensation.
I sit in the dark and I think.

I came here to find the truth about my father's death. I found it. My father was not a victim. He was not a man undone by circumstance or tragedy or the slow erosion of grief. He was an architect. He designed a machine — a beautiful, meticulous, well-organized machine — and its purpose was to destroy women. Not because he hated them. Because he believed, with the sincere conviction of every monster in history, that he was helping. That controlled adversity made the twins stronger. That the game was for their benefit.

He was wrong. The game was for the house. *The game exists because it exists. It is the thing that gives the house purpose.* He wrote that himself. He knew, on some level, that the justification was hollow. The game wasn't teaching the twins resilience. The game was consuming people to sustain itself, like a fire that burns wood to keep burning, and Peter was the man who kept stacking logs.
And now Peter is dead, and the game is still running.

I'm the sixth player. I'm in Round 5. Dependency. Next is Violence. And after that — nothing. The game resets. It starts again with a new governess and a new cycle of carefully calibrated trauma. It keeps going because it was designed to keep going. There's no off switch. There's no end condition. There's just the next round and the next player and the next and the next.
Unless.
The word forms in the dark. Small and sharp, like a splinter working its way out of skin.
Unless.

I open the laptop. The screen floods the room with light and I flinch, then don't. I pull up the file structure again. The folders. The rounds. The rules. The meticulous, sociopathic, beautiful architecture of the Game.
Games are systems. Systems have rules. Rules have dependencies. Dependencies have failure points.

Peter wrote it down. All of it. Every rule, every contingency, every protocol. Forty-seven pages of design document. Subfolders of incident logs and psychological profiles and exit strategies. He documented his machine down to the bolt. He was proud of it. He wanted it preserved.
He wanted it preserved.

That means it's here — all of it. Not just the record, but the operating system. The rules engine. The thing that makes the game *go*. And anything that can be documented can be altered. Anything that can be coded can be recoded.
*The game exists because it exists.*
But a game only exists as long as someone agrees to play it.
I pull up a blank document. I start typing.
I type: *New rules.*
